It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: REGULATION & GOVERNANCE FORUM Orchestrating sustainability: The case of European Union biofuel governance Philip Schleifer London School of Economics and Political Science, Department of International Relations, Grantham Research Institute on Climate Change and the Environment, London, UK Abstract This article provides an empirical analysis of orchestration – that is, the initiation, support, and embrace- ment of private governance arrangements through public regulators – in the ﬁeld of European Union biofuel governance. It examines the emerging sustainability regime and shows that orchestration has been extensively practiced. Regulators in the European Union have used a range of directive and facilitative measures to initiate and support private biofuel certiﬁcation schemes and to incorporate them in their regulatory frameworks. This has given rise to a hybrid regime in which public and private approaches are closely intertwined. Discussing the beneﬁts and complications of engaging with private biofuel sustainability governance, the article’s ﬁndings point to a partial failure of orchestration in this policy area. Keywords: biofuels, European Union, orchestration, private governance, transnational regulation. 1. Introduction Since the Rio Earth Summit in 1992 and the proclamation of Agenda 21, the concept of sustainable development has been at the center of international environmental politics. But making sustainability work under conditions of globalization poses difﬁcult challenges. In the countries of the south, weak administrative capacities are among the main obstacles for achiev- ing sustainability goals. And in the north, where states are strong, regulators remain constrained by national borders and, therefore, ﬁnd it difﬁcult to regulate transnational production net- works.At the same time, states are as reluctant as ever to confer authority to supranational bodies and many intergovernmental forums currently experience stalemates as a result of an increas- ingly heterogeneous international system. In order to overcome these obstacles, it has been argued that public regulators should harness the capacities and competencies of private sustainability governance (PSG) (Abbott 2012). The term PSG refers to the plethora of industry schemes (e.g. Global Sustainable Elec- tricity Partnership), civil society initiatives (e.g. Rainforest Alliance), and multi-stakeholder arrangements (e.g. Forest Stewardship Council) that have emerged in this area in recent years. In a series of articles, Abbott and Snidal (2009, 2010, 2012) argue that public regulators should actively support and embrace these arrangements. They call this “orchestration” and believe that it would strengthen the international regulatory system and help reaching sustainability goals. However, as of yet, this literature remains largely conceptual and normative and there is little real-world evidence about the beneﬁts and potential complications of orchestration. The article proceeds in four steps. The next section brieﬂy introduces the concept of orches- tration. This is followed by some background information about EU biofuel policies and gov- ernance. Distinguishing between directive and facilitative orchestration, it is then described how regulators in the EU initiated, supported, and embraced PSG schemes in their attempt to mitigate the environmental impact of their biofuel policies. In a ﬁnal section, the beneﬁts and complications of EU biofuel orchestration are discussed. 2. The case for orchestration In a recent article, Abbott and Snidal (2009, p. 511) argue: “states and intergovernmental organizations can and should more actively support and steer [PSG schemes], embracing them as valuable components of the international regulatory system.” They call this “orchestration” and believe that it would strengthen international regulation and at the same time mitigate some of the problems that currently undermine the legitimacy and effectiveness of PSG. The concept of orchestration is deeply rooted in New Public Management theory and the literature on New Domestic Governance. These approaches promote a shift away from central- ized administration toward the use of non-traditional, often private regulatory instruments. Key elements of the new public management agenda are deregulation, outsourcing regulatory func- tions to private actors, the use of market-based mechanisms, and the promotion of competition in the provision of public services (Lane 2000). Thus, calls for orchestration imply a new role for states and IGOs in global governance. In what could be dubbed old global governance, states and international organizations (IOs) were the primary and often only providers of regulation. Rulemaking took place at the national level, through international negotiations and within IOs. Rule implementation and enforcement mostly occurred through the state and its agencies as IOs typically lack direct enforcement powers. In old global governance, public regulators and the subjects of regulation were, thus, engaged in a direct relationship. In contrast, the “key to orchestration is to bring third parties into the governance arrangement” (Abbott et al. 2010, p. 1). The goal is to initiate, support, and coordinate PSG schemes and to integrate them in public regulatory frameworks rather than to govern directly. This is based on the premise that private actors possess resources and have developed competencies and expertise that public regulators can use to better reach their regulatory objectives (see Table 1). However, public and private arrangements often remain poorly aligned. The orchestration literature argues that closing this engagement gap would strengthen the international regulatory system in several ways. One important beneﬁt of orchestration is that governing via intermedi- aries demands fewer public resources. Another advantage is the mobility and reach of PSG. Intergovernmental policymaking processes are often unwieldy and time-consuming and states remain constrained by the rules of the international free trade regime and the transnational nature of production. In this situation, PSG could help to ﬁll in gaps in the international regulatory system and to improve the extraterritorial impact of public regulation (Abbott & Snidal 2009, p. 564). At the same time, states and IOs could use their authority to boost the effectiveness and legitimacy of PSG. One important factor that limits the effectiveness of PSG as a system is its fragmentation and heterogeneity. PSG is the result of the uncoordinated interactions of thou- Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 534 Page 3: sands of groups and organizations from business and civil society. This spontaneous and decen- tralized evolution has produced many gaps and overlaps in terms of issue and industry coverage. As a result, many schemes focus on sectors with highly branded, consumer-facing companies, whereas less visible industries have received considerably less attention. The high concentration of PSG schemes in some sectors is also problematic. The existence of multiple schemes invites forum shopping by companies and increases search and information costs (Abbott 2012, p. 558). PSG’s legitimacy problems are partly a result of the fact that it does not conform to accepted notions of representative democracy. In the transnational realm in which these schemes operate, no clearly deﬁned demos or principal exists with the ability to hold them to account. To generate legitimacy, the concept of deliberative democracy with its focus on participation and transpar- ency has been proposed as an alternative. However, PSG schemes strongly vary in these dimen- sions of institutional design (Abbott 2012, pp. 559–560; Fransen 2012). Abbott and Snidal argue that orchestration can help to mitigate these problems. They propose a number of measures that they subdivide into directive and facilitative orchestration. Directive orchestration relies on the authority of the state. In this regard, states can scale up exemplary initiatives through formally incorporating them in their regulatory frameworks. Furthermore, they can mandate baseline principles, transparency requirements, and other insti- tutional parameters for schemes operating in their jurisdictions. In this way, they can inﬂuence their composition, form, and procedures and, thus, maximize their participatory character and reduce the risk of capture (Abbott & Snidal 2009, pp. 565–573). However, in the transnational realm directive orchestration is often not possible. In particular, IOs often lack direct enforce- ment powers. Through a range of facilitative measures they can still act as orchestrators. Facili- tation is “softer” than directive orchestration and makes use of the full range of governance tools that are characteristic of the New Public Management agenda. For example, as facilitators, orchestrators can provide ﬁnancial support and technical assistance to PSG schemes. They can also endorse initiatives they deem as exemplary and, thus, provide them with legitimacy. In addition to material and ideational support, they can also participate in the formation of new initiatives and, thus, inﬂuence their norms, structures, and procedures (Abbott & Snidal 2009, pp. 573–577). However, as of yet, little is known about real-world orchestration, its beneﬁts, and potential complications. In order to address this gap, the remainder of this article describes and discusses the case of EU biofuel governance, a policy ﬁeld in which public regulators have closely engaged with PSG. 3. Biofuel policies and governance in the European Union In the early 2000s, policymakers in the EU started to promote biofuels as a means to reach greenhouse gas (GHG) emission reduction targets, foster energy security, and stimulate devel- opment in rural areas at home and abroad (European Commission 2005). Over the last decade, these policies created a biofuel market with an estimated volume of 14,041 Ktoe or 4.65 percent of total transport fuels (USDA Foreign Agricultural Service 2011, pp. 1–2). With the adoption of Directive 2009/28, also known as the EU Renewable Energy Directive (EU RED), this volume is set to increase to 10 percent by 2020 (European Union 2009).1 However, opinions strongly diverge as to the sustainability of biofuel production. One major issue raised in this debate relates to the carbon intensity of biofuels. The EU promotes biofuels as a means to reduce GHG emissions and, thus, to reach climate change reduction targets. But ofﬁcial ﬁgures often do not consider emissions emanating from direct and indirect land use change. Direct and indirect land use changes are problematic as they can result in an overall negative GHG balance of biofuels. This is the case when, for example, forests, peatlands, or wetlands are converted (Nufﬁeld Council on Bioethics 2011, pp. 32–33). The social impact of biofuels has also become a highly contentious issue. Besides poor labor standards in producer countries and problems with land grabbing, the food crisis of 2007/2008, in particular, sparked much debate about biofuels. As food prices increased sharply during this period, Jean Ziegel, the United Nations Special Rapporteur on the Right to Food, even called them a “crime against humanity” and requested a ﬁve year moratorium on their production (Borger 2008). EU regulators responded to these pressures and criticism by including environmental and, to a lesser extent, social safeguards in their policies. Most signiﬁcant in this regard is the EU RED. The directive contains a sustainability scheme that all biofuels produced or imported to the EU must meet in order to count against the European target. The following subsections provide a detailed description of the emerging public–private biofuel regime. 3.1. European Union level At the EU level, the ﬁrst signiﬁcant step to promote biofuels was Directive 2003/30 on the promotion of the use of biofuels or other renewable fuels for transport (European Union 2003). The directive set an indicative, non-mandatory, blending target of 5.75 percent to be reached by 2010. However, the industry and member states responded only reluctantly to the directive. To further promote the cause of biofuels, the Commission, therefore, formulated the EU Strategy for Biofuels in 2006 and the Renewable Energy Road Map in 2007 (European Commission 2006, 2007). The latter proposed a binding target for the use of biofuels in the transport sector of 10 percent to be reached by all member states by 2020. The European Council endorsed the Commission’s proposal, but demanded that biofuels used for blending need to be produced sustainably (European Council 2007). This and the intensifying controversy surrounding biofuels put pressure on the Commission to amend its proposal. In January 2008, the Commis- sion presented a draft directive in which mandatory sustainability criteria for biofuels were ﬁrst Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 536 Page 5: mentioned (European Commission 2008). Finding the support of the Council and the European Parliament, the proposal was formally adopted as Directive 2009/28 the following year (European Union 2009). Thus, besides establishing a 10 percent target for renewable energy in the transport sector, EU RED also contains a mandatory sustainability scheme. The scheme stipulates that biofuels need to achieve a minimum level of 35 percent savings in GHG emissions when compared to fossil fuels. Another criterion mandates that biofuels cannot be obtained from land that was categorized in January 2008 as having a high biodiversity value. In the same way, biomass obtained from land with high carbon stock cannot be used for biofuel production. It is notice- able, however, that EU RED does not include any social criteria, such as labor and land use rights in its sustainability scheme. Also, food security and indirect land use change are not part of the mandatory criteria. EU RED merely requires the Commission to report to the European Parlia- ment and the Council every two years about the impact of biofuel production on social sustainability and food prices (European Union 2009, pp. 36–38). 3.2. Member state level At the member state level, several governments created national schemes to promote the use of biofuels and to implement the European targets. In the following, the examples of Britain and Germany are brieﬂy discussed.2 In Britain, the government ﬁrst announced its plans to introduce a national blending mandate for biofuels in the transport sector in 2005. Three years later, the Renewable Transport Fuels Obligation (RTFO) came into force. The RTFO established an initial blending target of 2.5 percent for 2008/2009, set to increase to ﬁve percent by 2010/2011. Furthermore, it required fuel suppliers to report on a list of sustainability criteria, very similar to those listed in the EU RED, but also including workers’ rights and land rights (ECOFYS 2007, pp. 6–15). From April 2008, all fuel suppliers in the United Kingdom supplying 450,000 liters or more per annum were obliged to report on the volume of biofuels supplied, as well as their sustainability and carbon intensity. In December 2011, the RTFO was amended to include the mandatory sustainability require- ments set out in EU RED (Department of Transport website). Until 2006, the German government mainly relied on tax exemptions to promote the pro- duction and use of biofuels. It then switched policy instruments and, like Britain, introduced a mandatory quota for biofuels. The Biofuel Quota Act of 2006 mandates an annual 0.25 percent increase in the share of biofuels until a total share of eight percent is reached. Non-compliance is sanctioned through a fee on every liter missing to reach the annual quota (FNR website-a). To implement the European sustainability scheme, the Federal Ministry of Food and Agriculture complemented the Biofuel Quota Act with the Biomass Sustainability Ordinance and the Biofuel Sustainability Ordinance. From 2010 onwards, these policies made the EU RED’s sustainability criteria mandatory for all biofuels produced and used within Germany (FNR website-b, c). 3.3. Private sustainability schemes Alongside the public regulatory framework, about a dozen private biomass certiﬁcation schemes have emerged in the European biofuel industry. Table 2 provides a list of those schemes that have obtained formal EU recognition as qualifying standards (a procedure which will be described in further detail). Initiated by single companies, industry associations, or through multi-stakeholder processes, these schemes have developed principles and criteria for sustainable biofuel production and set up veriﬁcation and certiﬁcation procedures. Essentially, Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 537 Page 6: these systems function as clubs (Potoski & Prakash 2009). Firms striving for membership have to implement the club’s code of conduct in their operations. With the help of professional auditing ﬁrms, club managers then control corporate compliance along the biofuel supply chain. Compliant operators are issued a certiﬁcate that they can use to signal their sustain- ability performance to EU authorities and other relevant external audiences. Among the PSG schemes in the European biofuel industry are industry initiatives like the Biomass Biofuel Sustainability Voluntary Scheme (2BSvs), and multi-stakeholder initiatives like the Roundtable on Sustainable Biofuels (RSB). Some schemes have been explicitly designed to serve as certiﬁers for the European biofuel market, whereas others only later developed standards for biofuel production. Also, there are differences regarding the content of private standards. Whereas all schemes cover the environmental criteria laid out in the EU RED (e.g. a minimum level of GHG emission savings when compared with fossil fuels, a prohibition of biomass obtained from land categorized as having a high biodiversity value or high carbon stock), others include social criteria and, thereby, go beyond EU RED. For example, besides basic labor rights (e.g. right to collective bargaining, prohibition of forced labor), the RSB standard also includes criteria in the areas of land rights, food security, and sustainable livelihoods (German & Schoneveld 2011). 4. It shows that the Commission, member states, and IOs actively supported, facilitated, and embraced private biomass certiﬁcation schemes. Directive measures In the case of EU biofuel governance, public regulators have taken directive measures at both the EU and the member state levels. To implement the sustainability criteria set out in EU RED, the Commission adopted what Lin (2012) calls a meta-standard approach. Under this approach, economic operators can demonstrate compliance with the EU’s sustainability scheme by becom- ing certiﬁed by a PSG scheme that is recognized as a qualifying standard. A qualifying standard is a scheme that is considered as equivalent to the EU RED’s sustainability criteria. In order to become recognized as a qualifying standard, PSG schemes have to undergo a recognition pro- cedure in which their standards and auditing systems are assessed. Amongst other criteria, a successful application requires independent third party monitoring, a sound documentation system, and evidence of an appropriate skill level of the auditors. Furthermore, it is examined whether a scheme can serve as a source of data on food security and indirect land use change. In July 2011, the Commission concluded the ﬁrst application cycle and formally recognized Biomass Biofuels Sustainability Voluntary Scheme (2BSvs), Bonsucro, Greenergy, International Sustainability and Carbon Certiﬁcation (ISCC), Abengoa RED Bioenergy Sustainability Assur- ance (RBSA), Round Table on Responsible Soy (RTRS), and Roundtable on Sustainable Biomaterials (RSB) as qualifying standards. In 2012, ﬁve further private schemes (Ensus, REDcert, Red Tractor, Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil [RSPO], and Scottish Quality Farm Assured Combinable Crops [SQC]) were granted recognition (European Commission website). Economic operators can now use these schemes and their certiﬁcates to demonstrate compliance with EU RED. A second way to demonstrate compliance is through the member states’ national control systems (European Commission 2010, p. 2). PSG schemes have also become an essential part of the regulatory system at the member state level. For example, the RTFO in Britain adopted a meta-standard approach very similar to that of EU RED. In this regard, the Renewable Fuels Agency, which until 2011 was charged with implementing the RTFO, recognized a number of PSG schemes as qualifying standards (Renewable Fuels Agency 2011, p. 39). Similar procedures exist in Germany and the Netherlands. Both countries have now recognized the PSG schemes assessed by the Commission, but have also developed national assessment protocols (Ministry of Economic Affairs 2011). For example, Germany assessed and recognized two PSG schemes (ISCC and REDcert) with a strong national focus a year before the Commission had even concluded its ﬁrst application round. 4.2. Facilitative measures One illustrative example of facilitative orchestration in the biofuel industry is the case of the ISCC. In its founding phase, this scheme was largely funded by the Agency for Renew- able Resources, a special branch of the German Ministry of Food and Agriculture (Federal Ministry of Food and Agriculture 2010). In a similar way, the RTRS has received public grants from the Swiss Secretariat for Economic Affairs (RTRS 2008). IOs have also ﬁnancially sup- ported PSG schemes operating in the biofuel industry. Through its Biodiversity and Agricul- ture Commodities Program, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), for example, provided grants of EUR 214,000 and EUR 71,000 to the RTRS and the RSPO respectively (IFC website). Besides the provision of ﬁnancial support, government agencies and IOs have actively participated in the formation and standard-setting process of some of the schemes. In this regard, the minutes of the RSB Steering Board suggest that the Swiss Federal Ofﬁce of Energy and the United Nations Environment Programme have been active participants of the RSB Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 539 Page 8: process (e.g. RSB 2006). The RSB is not the only voluntary scheme with public participation. The IFC, for example, is a founding member of Bonsucro, and the German Association for Development Cooperation (GIZ) supported the RTRS in its standard-setting activities (GIZ website). In sum, public regulators have used a variety of orchestration measures in the biofuel industry. To implement their sustainability standards, the Commission and several member states adopted a meta-standard approach and formally incorporated PSG schemes in their regulatory frameworks. They also acted as facilitators through participating in some of the schemes and through the provision of ﬁnancial support. 5. Discussion Its supporters argue that orchestration can increase the efﬁciency and reach of regulation and, at the same time, mitigate some of the problems currently undermining the effectiveness and legitimacy of PSG. Using these objectives as a yardstick, this ﬁnal section discusses the beneﬁts and complications of orchestration in the case of European biofuel governance. 5.1. Reducing costs and increasing reach? Probably the primary motivation for EU regulators to engage with PSG was the prospect of saving time and resources. In this regard, a study commissioned by the British Department for Transport discussing the beneﬁts of pursuing a meta-standard approach details: “The fact that proper standards are available and duplication is undesirable thus forms the main reason for choosing to build upon existing standards in a Meta-Standard. In addition to this, it would be impossible to develop a completely new standard within the short time frame available which would still be credible” (ECOFYS 2007, pp. 23–24). At a ﬁrst glance, the path taken appears to have delivered on this objective. After the adoption of EU RED in 2009, it took the Commission only two years to complete the ﬁrst application round for PSG schemes. Almost fully privately ﬁnanced, PSG schemes are now the primary implementation mechanism for EU sustainability regulation in this area. It goes without saying that the adoption of a more traditional command- and-control approach would have required considerably more time and public resources. However, it would be a mistake to assume that private biofuel certiﬁcation does not involve any costs. The development of a standard and certiﬁcation system typically amounts to several million euros. For example, the operational budget of the RTRS for the years 2007–2009 amounted to 1,780,000 EUR, and Bonsucro required some 400,000 EUR to ﬁnance its activities in 2006 (Bonsucro 2006, p. 23; RTRS 2007, p. 7). Given the large number of PSG schemes currently operating in the biofuel industry, their founding and operational costs are likely to be signiﬁcant. Of course, some of these schemes already existed before EU RED came into force. However, many others, for example, 2BSvs, Ensus, Greenergy, ISCC, RBSA, and REDcert, have explicitly been designed to function as certiﬁers for the European biofuel market. This raises questions about the macro-efﬁciency of EU biofuel governance. It appears that the Commis- sion’s decision to recognize any scheme “regardless of whether another recognized scheme already covers the same type of feedstock, area, etc.” has led to the creation of double structures and, as a result, to a highly fragmented system of PSG in this sector (European Commission 2010, p. 2). A closely related aspect concerns the distributional consequences of private biofuel certiﬁ- cation. The meta-standard approach has reduced the costs of regulation for the regulating agencies, but it did not eliminate them. In fact, it seems that most of the operational costs are Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 540 Page 9: borne by ﬁrms located at the upstream end of the supply chain (i.e. producers). Furthermore, the highly fragmented biofuel certiﬁcation market is likely to generate search and information costs for upstream producers. Taken together, these costs could be signiﬁcant and concerns have been raised that they could create barriers of entry for new and small-scale producers in developing countries (Lee et al. 2011; Pacini & Assunção 2011). Of course, similar costs might have arisen under a purely public system. For instance, various national standards would have resulted in high search and information costs for target producers. Making a statement about which system would have fared better is difﬁcult, as this would require counterfactuals. Still, it appears that EU orchestrators could have done a better job by, for example, limiting the number of private standards and, thus, the degree of fragmentation. With regard to the extraterritorial implementation of EU sustainability regulation, relying on PSG schemes has clearly facilitated the extraterritorial impact of EU sustainability regulation. Unconstrained by national borders, private auditing companies are able to monitor compliance in the industry’s transnational production networks. This makes the extraterritorial implemen- tation of EU RED possible, even in countries with low administrative capacities. For example, as of today, the ISCC system has certiﬁed operations in more than 30 countries, among them many developing countries (ISCC website). In contrast, cross-border inspections through the EU or its member states would have been more difﬁcult, if not impossible, to achieve. 5.2. Making private sustainability governance (PSG) more effective and legitimate? The EU’s decision to adopt a meta-standard approach put the Commission in a strong position to shape the emerging system of PSG in the biofuel industry. Its energy section developed an assessment protocol, and in 2010 the recognition procedure was launched. As it was clear from the beginning that only accredited schemes would be able to function as certiﬁers under EU RED, PSG scheme sponsors had a strong incentive to apply. This allowed the Commission to impose a set of baseline requirements. However, it did not set the bar very high. As a result, biomass certiﬁcation schemes continue to differ in signiﬁcant ways. For example, strong differ- ences persist with regard to stakeholder inclusion. Whereas the roundtable initiatives (Bonsucro, RTRS, RSB, and RSPO) involve a broad range of stakeholders in their governance and standard- setting activities, other schemes are signiﬁcantly less inclusive. Above all, this is true for the industry-led initiatives (Ministry of Economic Affairs 2012, p. 24). Strong differences also persist with regard to standards. For instance, German and Schoneveld (2011, pp. 10–14) ﬁnd that – to varying degrees – RSB, Bonsucro, Greenergy, ISCC, and the RTRS include social criteria in their standards, whereas 2BSvs and RBSA contain no such provisions. Furthermore, there is evidence suggesting that PSG schemes continue to differ in their auditing practices. In this regard, the RSB and the RTRS rely primarily on on-site ﬁeld audits, REDcert and ISCC make extensive use of self-declaration,3 and the 2BSvs system even allows for desk audits where a decision about compliance or non-compliance is made on the basis of documentation only. The latter practice is generally considered as problematic, especially in light of evidence sug- gesting that “an audit by 2BSvs can in practice be quite easily fully performed with deskwork with no visit to the ﬁeld for real veriﬁcation – also in high-risk areas” (Ministry of Economic Affairs 2012, p. 28). Differences in stakeholder engagement, standard coverage, and auditing practices are prob- lematic for another reason. By failing to establish a level playing ﬁeld among PSG schemes, EU regulators have created opportunities for forum shopping and, thus, contributed to the onset of adverse competition. In this regard, a comparison of the market uptake of the four multi- feedstock schemes granted recognition in the 2011 application cycle suggests that the more Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 541 Page 10: lenient schemes do considerably better. As of June 2013, 2BSvs had issued 618, REDcert 1224, and ISCC 2801 certiﬁcates (2BSvs website; ISCC website; REDcert website). In contrast, the more comprehensive and inclusive RSB appears to experience problems on the marketplace. Within the same time frame, this scheme only issued seven certiﬁcates (RSB Services website). There are many reasons for slow market uptake, but competition from schemes with weaker standards, laxer veriﬁcation procedures, and lower costs is likely to be a major factor. A closer look at the case of the RSB illustrates a major shortcoming of EU biofuel orches- tration. The RSB is often referred to as the gold standard of biofuel certiﬁcation schemes, praised for its comprehensiveness, inclusiveness, and transparency (Nufﬁeld Council on Bioethics 2011, p. xxvi). In other words, it is the kind of initiative the orchestration literature would want to see scaled up and supported by public regulators. However, in the biofuel case orchestrators have done the opposite. By setting a relatively low baseline for PSG schemes (e.g. making no requirements for stakeholder inclusion and making no references to social stan- dards), they endorsed a model of private governance that beneﬁted initiatives like 2BSvs, ISCC, and REDcert. Legitimized by formal EU recognition, these schemes were able to establish themselves as dominant players in the biofuel certiﬁcation market. This put pressure on the RSB and the scheme is currently considering the introduction of a less stringent entry-level version of its standard. The RSB secretariat justiﬁes this move with:“It is hoped that the 2-tiered approach would avoid the prospect that too many operators decide to get certiﬁed for com- pliance with the EU RED through other certiﬁcation schemes” (RSB 2010, p. 12). This anecdote is indicative of a negative dynamic among PSG schemes in the biofuel industry. It appears that, by setting the bar too low, orchestrators have undercut, rather than promoted, good private governance in this area. 6. Conclusion This article described and discussed the practice of orchestration in the European biofuel industry. Beginning in the early 2000s, EU policymakers introduced blending quotas for biofuels and, in response to concerns about their social and environmental impact, ﬂanked these policies with a set of mandatory sustainability criteria. For implementation, EU regulators primarily rely on private certiﬁcation schemes that they facilitated, supported, and incorporated in their regulatory frameworks. Summing up the above discussion, the case at hand suggests that engaging with PSG schemes can indeed be valuable and allow public regulators to realize some of the beneﬁts discussed in the orchestration literature. In this regard, orchestration provided EU regulators with a cost-efﬁcient implementation mechanism. No resources had to be used to create a public control system and synergies could be realized through relying on existing initiatives. In addition, embracing PSG schemes has facilitated the extraterritorial implementation of EU sustainability regulation. However, the above discussion also revealed that, in other areas, EU orchestration has performed poorly. Most importantly, EU regulators have failed to establish a level playing ﬁeld among biofuel certiﬁcation schemes. This has created opportunities for forum shopping and has trig- gered adverse competition. As a result, orchestration appears to have undercut rather than promoted good private governance in the biofuel industry. One possible explanation for this partial “orchestration failure” is the activities of well-organized economic interest groups. There is evidence suggesting that key producer countries and their industry associations have been very active at the EU level, lobbying the Commission to exclude certain criteria from its mandatory sustainability scheme (CEO 2008).Also, business groups within the EU have proven very capable Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 542 Page 11: of networking and organizing around public biofuel regulation. Learning about the Commis- sion’s plans to rely on PSG in this area, the major players in the European agriculture industry were quick to develop standards and certiﬁcation systems of their own and to lobby the Com- mission for their recognition.4 Of course, this is only a very tentative explanation and more empirical research is needed to investigate the matter further. More generally, the hitherto largely conceptual and normative orchestration literature would beneﬁt from studies with a strong empirical focus. In particular, important insights could be gained from systematically comparing the causes, mechanisms, and outcomes of orchestration across different policy areas, as well as different types of orchestrators (e.g. states and IOs).
